definition: To carefully select, organize, and present a collection of things — choosing only what's most valuable, relevant, or meaningful from a larger pool. It implies taste, judgment, and intentionality, not just gathering.
example_sentences: She curated a playlist for the road trip.
The director curated an exhibition featuring works from twelve countries around a single theme.
usage_notes: Exploded in everyday usage in the digital age — people now curate social media feeds, playlists, newsletters, even outfits. One common mistake: overusing it for simple gathering. Reserve it for situations where selective judgment is the point. Noun form: curation; the person: curator.
memory_tip: Think of a museum curator — someone whose whole job is deciding what's worth showing and what stays in the basement. When you curate anything, you're being that curator: a gatekeeper of quality.

definition: To make a process, action, or outcome easier or smoother — often by removing obstacles, providing support, or helping things move forward without doing the work yourself.
example_sentences: The manager's job was to facilitate, not to micromanage.
The new software was designed to facilitate collaboration between remote teams across different time zones.
usage_notes: Common in business, education, and formal writing. A facilitator runs workshops or meetings without imposing their own views — they guide the process, not the outcome. Don't confuse with 'enable' (more neutral) or 'allow' (more passive). 'Facilitate' implies active effort to smooth the way.
memory_tip: The root is Latin 'facilis' — easy. To facilitate is literally to make something easy. Think: facility (a place that makes things easy to do) shares the same root.

definition: To be made up of; to consist of. The whole comprises its parts — the larger thing contains or includes the smaller elements within it.
example_sentences: The committee comprises five elected members.
The course comprises twelve weekly lectures, three written assignments, and a final exam worth half the grade.
usage_notes: One of the most commonly misused words in English. The whole comprises the parts — never 'comprised of'. Wrong: 'The team is comprised of five players.' Right: 'The team comprises five players' or 'is composed of five players.' Avoid 'comprised of' in formal writing entirely.
memory_tip: The whole COMPRISES the parts — just like a container contains what's inside it. The big thing does the comprising, never the small parts.

definition: The quality of being honest and having strong moral principles — doing the right thing even when no one is watching. Also refers to the state of being whole, complete, and uncorrupted (e.g., structural integrity).
example_sentences: She acted with integrity even when it cost her.
He turned down the bribe without hesitation, knowing his integrity was worth more than any short-term gain.
usage_notes: Used in two main contexts: moral (honesty, ethical wholeness) and technical (data integrity, structural integrity). In everyday speech it almost always means the moral sense. Common collocations: 'act with integrity', 'a person of integrity', 'compromise one's integrity.' A compliment that carries real weight — don't overuse it casually.
memory_tip: Think of 'integer' — a whole number, nothing missing, nothing broken. Integrity means you are whole and undivided: your actions match your values, every time.
